sources.title: Paradise Lost

sources.bibliographic_note: Public-domain epic; reference for Satan's rebellion and infernal imagery alluded to throughout Moby-Dick.

sources.title: Pandects (Roman law)
sources.author: Britannica Editors
sources.bibliographic_note: Reference support for the Pandects (Digest) as part of Justinian's codification of Roman law.
sources.source_note: Britannica page verified 2026-06-08: the Pandects are a collection of passages from Roman jurists in 50 books, commissioned by Justinian in 530 and published 533 CE as part of his codification of Roman law.

sources.title: Samuel Johnson (English author and lexicographer)
sources.author: Britannica Editors
sources.bibliographic_note: Reference support for Samuel Johnson as lexicographer of A Dictionary of the English Language (1755).
sources.source_note: Britannica page verified 2026-06-08: Samuel Johnson (1709-1784), English lexicographer and man of letters, compiler of A Dictionary of the English Language; page shows his dictionary entries.
sources.license_note: External reference page; cite for verification only, no extended reuse of Britannica prose.

notes.anchor: So in dreams, have I seen majestic Satan thrusting forth his tormented colossal claw from the flame Baltic of Hell
notes.note: The image of Satan reaching a colossal claw out of a fiery hell echoes Milton's Paradise Lost, where the fallen archangel rears up from the burning lake. Melville offers this Miltonic vision as the dark counterpart to a serene, Isaiah-inspired view of the whale's tail, two opposed literary lenses for the same sublime sight.

notes.anchor: surpasses Justinian’s Pandects and the Bylaws of the Chinese Society for the Suppression of Meddling
notes.note: The Pandects (or Digest) were the vast compilation of Roman legal opinions that Emperor Justinian commissioned in 530 and issued in 533 CE, running to fifty books and forming the core of European civil law. Melville's joke is that the whalemen's two-line code outdoes that monumental legal system in sheer terse comprehensiveness.

notes.anchor: hunted for thousands of years, by Semiramis, by Porus, by Hannibal
notes.note: These names stand for ancient commanders who fielded war elephants: Hannibal, the Carthaginian general, most famously crossed the Alps in 218 BCE with an army that included at least 37 elephants to invade Rome. Melville lists such elephant-hunters across millennia to argue that elephants have survived long hunting, so the whale will too.

notes.anchor: I have invariably used a huge quarto edition of Johnson
notes.note: Samuel Johnson (1709-1784) compiled A Dictionary of the English Language (1755), the standard English dictionary for a century. Ishmael's joke is that Johnson's own bulk makes his huge quarto a fitting authority for so vast a subject as the whale, a massive lexicographer to define a massive animal.
